42iSN6RHLoc.text: A video message from Pamela Witcher, CAV’s Community Relations Manager  Hello! My name is Pamela Witcher, Community Relations Manager. My responsibilities include managing a new team of Community Outreach Specialists that work with me to raise awareness and spread information about Canada VRS far and wide. Our geographic residences are between both coast sides; some from the West, some from the East and some from the middle. To know who they are, you can view the SRV Canada VRS Facebook Group. As a community, please welcome them and work with us! Our community outreach work is strongly tied with the work of Customer Service Representatives who are located in Montreal and provide Customer Service and Technical Support, known as 9050. Community outreach and Customer Service always partner and work together. Over time, Canadians are beginning to understand different tasks offered by both fields of expertise; the community outreach to educate and raise awareness by sharing information, and the customer service to provide technical and customer support. Working together, we are able to fulfill the needs of the Users and potential users in their own regions by providing information, answers, and technical support through sign languages. It is common, after outreach events, for VRS Users to express appreciation for our presence, and the access to sign languages to obtain answers and assistance, and we love the positive feedback! Since Canada VRS is still new in Canada, our number 1 priority goes to the Deaf and Hard of Hearing Canadians of all ages and groups who can benefit from using Canada VRS. This includes families of Deaf and Hard of Hearing children. Now, I will explain how the outreach events are organized to make each event efficient and successful. To host regional events, it works both ways. Either the local services, organizations, schools, or individual users contact us to make it happen, or we contact them to make it happen. While at an event, we emphasize the importance of partnerships and collaborations between local services, organizations, schools and Deaf community to make the in-person or remote events collective and successful. Each event usually takes 3 months of time to coordinate availabilities between specialists, representatives and local community, as well as to coordinate a room, and a flyer to mutually develop and share on various levels of distribution: community-based collaborators, our website and Facebook Group or individual shares. We also go to already-organized events such as Deaf Expos to set up booths, and those that are organized by and for, specific Deaf Canadian sub-groups such as LGBT, Indigenous People, Senior Citizens, etc. In addition to the strategies mentioned, we have also developed materials sent out to organizations, schools and services to see how we can work together to share Canada VRS. In a letter it offers a “Click Me” button that could be embedded in a website and shared with interested viewers. For the family-related events, camps, centres and schools, we have also developed a parent letter to raise awareness among families by explaining what Canada VRS is, and that the child will need a consent form from the parents or guardians to obtain a VRS number, to be empowered and enjoy VRS. So, you can see how our outreach strategies vary between requests, needs, and events. Outreaching is one of the ways that help us identify specific strengths to strengthen and barriers to overcome. Yes, that’s right. If you are interested in getting a “Click Me” button for your organization, your community’s website or to discuss hosting an event, please feel free to contact me: pwitcher@srvcanadavrs.ca.

rnPk3pLVQ6w.text: Call for Testimonials Are you a past or current client, camper, volunteer or staff member of Silent Voice?  We want to know how Silent Voice impacted your life!  Visit our site to share your experiences: https://silentvoice.ca/2019/03/27/share-your-experiences-with-silent-voice/ Silent Voice has been involved in the Deaf community and providing support since 1975! We offer many programs and services, including: Family and Children Programs Deaf Adult Services Parent Education Program Infant Hearing Program (ASL Services) Volunteer Program Settlement Services, and more! Now, we need your support. How? We're collecting stories from the community about Silent Voice. Whether you are a past or current client, camper, volunteer or staff member, everyone is invited to share their stories! We want to hear your memories of Silent Voice. You can send photos and video messages too! You can sign or speak - we'll add captions for you! Tell us how you feel about Silent Voice, or share your favourite memory! Once we've collected your messages, photos and videos, we will include them in presentations, promotional materials, on our website and our social media. Do you want to get involved? Click the link to visit our site and fill out the form to submit your comments, photos and videos. We look forward to your stories about Silent Voice!

sNIord5i1WA.text: ASL SRV Canada VRS  Update - Spring 2017  Hello. I’m Sue Decker, Executive Director of CAV, and I have some exciting news to share with you about our plans in 2017. First, I am pleased to tell you we will be increasing VRS hours during weekdays (Monday to Friday). Beginning on April 3rd, SRV Canada VRS will be open from 9 AM to midnight (eastern time), expanding VRS for 3 more hours! Secondly, I want to let you know our team is growing too. We’ve recently added a new position called, “Community Relations Specialist”. Her name is Pamela Witcher, and, many of you may already know her. Pamela is responsible to get out and meet with VRS users across Canada to exchange ideas, collect feedback and respond to your questions. Now, I would like to introduce you to Pamela. Hi, I’m Pamela Witcher, and I am thrilled to be joining the team and looking forward to engaging with all of you. Of course, Canada is a large country so it may take some time for me to get to your area. 8lilMlJMCeo.text: ASL Press Release - 15 December 2017 - FINAL  December 15, 2017 Press Release Deaf, Deaf-Blind and Hard of Hearing (DDBHH) Wireless Accessibility Committee (WAC) release TWO videos, both in ASL and LSQ Purpose of both videos is about the Wireless Code (WC) Release is from Ottawa, Canada Six (6) different Deaf, Deaf-Blind and Hard of Hearing (DDBHH) organizations are pleased to collaborate with CWTA Canadian Wireless Telecommunications Association (CWTA) Secondly, Canadian Radio-television Telecommunications Commission (CRTC) The purpose is release of accessible videos explaining the updated Wireless Code (WC) The new updated WC - Wireless Code - started on December 1, 2017 These videos are in ASL/LSQ and have English/French captions CWTA’s Wireless Accessibility Committee (WAC) included 3: 1) Wireless Service Providers, meaning the wireless companies; 2) CWTA staff, and 3) the following DDBHH individuals / organizations - six organizations - who represents and which organization? Lisa Anderson-Kellett, Chairperson, Deaf Wireless Canada Consultative Committee-Comite pour les Services Sans fil des Sourds du Canada (DWCC-CSSSC); Frank Folino, President, Canadian Association of the Deaf-Association des Sourds du Canada (CAD-ASC); Megan McHugh, President, Canadian National Society of Deaf-Blind (CNSDB); Elliott Richman, Assistant Director, Deafness Advocacy Association Nova Scotia (DAANS); Frank O’Sullivan, Executive Director, Society of Deaf and Hard of Hearing Nova Scotians (SDHHNS); Gary Malkowski, Vice-President, Stakeholder & Employer Relations, Canadian Hearing Society (CHS) DWCC made recommendations on how the “old Wireless Code” may be improved for DDBHH Canadians during the public hearing for CRTC TNC 2016-293 CRTC included our recommendations, videos in ASL/LSQ, about the updated Wireless Code 2.0: as described in Telecom Regulatory Policy (TRP) 2017-200: A Review of the Wireless Code That decision required Wireless Service Providers (WSPs) to consult with accessibility groups to produce 2 ASL/LSQ videos projects on 2 separate topics: 1) the updated Wireless Code (WC) 2) terminology (big words) seen in wireless contracts in ASL/LSQ The CRTC 2017-200 decision has three (3) requirements for the videos project in ASL/LSQ: 1) the videos provide information and are not to be used in companies’ advertising campaigns 2) the videos must be closed-captioned in English and French 3) CRTC, CWTA and all Wireless Service Providers must show the same videos on their company websites Also, all DDBHH organizations are encouraged to distribute these videos. What did collaboration with the CWTA look like? DWCC group liaised between all 6 organizations as collective DDBHH perspectives and lived experiences to CWTA when 1) developing video scripts, translate into ASL/LSQ 2) recommending ASL and LSQ signers 3) providing feedback during filming 4) post-production video editing The first videos describe the rights and responsibilities of wireless customers as described in the Wireless Code. You may WATCH the CWTA videos in ASL here: LSQ here: The six organizations look forward to supporting, advising and collaborating with the CWTA CWTA will be producing the ASL/LSQ terminology videos to be released in the summer of 2018 (next year) These videos goal purpose to help customers better understand their wireless contracts in ASL/LSQ. and Canadian Association of the Deaf (CAD-ASC) here at: Questions? You can contact us! Happy Holidays from all of us!! Organization descriptions follow: About Deaf Wireless Canada Consultative Committee (DWCC): The DWCC is a standing committee of the CAD-ASC and is a group of Deaf, Deaf-Blind, and Hard of Hearing consultants, analysts and committee volunteers across Canada. DWCC’s mandate is to advocate for equality for Deaf, DeafBlind and Hard of Hearing Canadians in wireless telecommunications as in: 1) Fair, uniform, cost reasonable wireless data plans for ASL and LSQ users. 2) Transparent and clear advertisement of plans offered. 3) Decreased disparity of wireless product and service provisions within the companies. 4) Promotion and availability of wireless software applications (apps) that ensure functional equivalency. 5) Accessible wireless emergency service provisions in Canada. For more information, visit www.deafwireless.ca Contact: lisa@deafwireless.ca The Canadian Association of the Deaf-Association des Sourds du Canada (CAD-ASC) is a not-for-profit organization as a national information, research and community action organization of Deaf people in Canada. Founded in 1940, CAD-ASC provides consultation and information on Deaf issues to the public, business, media, educators, governments and many others; CAD-ASC promotes and protects the rights, needs, and concerns of Deaf people who use American Sign Language (ASL) and langue des signes québécoise (LSQ). CAD-ASC is affiliated with the World Federation of the Deaf (WFD), and CAD-ASC is a United Nations-accredited Non-Governmental Organization (NGO) to the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities. For more information, visit www.cad.ca. Contact: ffolino@cad.ca The Canadian National Society of the Deaf-Blind, Inc. (CNSDB) was registered in 1985. CNSDB is a national consumer-run advocacy association dedicated to helping Canadians who are deaf-blind achieve a higher quality of life. We advocate for new and improved services across Canada. We also promote public awareness of deaf-blind issues and gather and distribute information in order to help empower individuals who are deaf-blind to become full participants of society. CNSDB provides expertise in accessibility related to the needs of individuals who are living with the distinct disability of deaf-blindness, which is different from deafness or blindness due to being unable to use one sense in order to compensate for the loss of the other. For more information, visit https://www.deafblindcanada.ca Hello! My name is Elliott Richman, and this is my sign name. I work for The Deafness Advocacy Association Nova Scotia (DAANS). DAANS was founded in 1976 and incorporated in 1978. Nova Scotia has an estimated 58,000 Deaf, hard of hearing, late deafened, and Deaf-blind Nova Scotians. The 58,000 people experience barriers in 6 areas: Communication access, education, employment, health, legal services, and recreation. DAANS works with the public, private, and nonprofit sectors to remove old barriers and prevent new barriers faced by the estimated 58,000 Nova Scotians. Contact: daans@ns.sympatico.ca Hello, my name is Frank O'Sullivan and I'm chair for Society of Deaf and Hard of Hearing Nova Scotians (SDHHNS). SDHHNS was incorporated in 1980 providing various programs and services across Nova Scotia for the approximately 58,000 Deaf, hard of hearing, deaf-blind and late deafened people. The goal is to gain full access to public, private and community services across Nova Scotia. For more information, visit  http://sdhhns.org/ Contact: fosullivan@ns.sympatico.ca What is CHS? Trusted since 1940, the Canadian Hearing Society provides industry-leading services and products that enable Deaf and hard of hearing Canadians to overcome barriers to participation. It is an independent, registered non-profit organization that reinvests proceeds from product and program sales back into community services, the focus of the organization. For more information about CHS services, visit www.chs.ca Contact: gmalkowski@chs.ca What is Canadian Wireless Telecommunications Association (CWTA)? CWTA is the authority on wireless issues, developments and trends in Canada. CWTA represents companies that provide services and products across the wireless sector. Representing the industry before all levels of government and various regulatory agencies, CWTA actively promotes the industry with the goal of ensuring continued growth of the wireless sector in Canada. CWTA administers a number of initiatives on behalf of its members, including corporate social responsibility programs and the national common short codes program. For more information, visit https://cwta.ca What is Canadian Radio-television Telecommunications Commission (CRTC)? CRTC is an administrative tribunal that regulates and supervises broadcasting and telecommunications in the public interest. The CRTC is dedicated to ensuring that Canadians — as citizens, creators and consumers — have access to a world-class communication system that promotes innovation and enriches their lives. For more information, visit http://crtc.gc.ca Two videos links shown: Thank you.

jm0DCg0UhZ4.text: ELIZABETH MORRIS  ♫ Hi, my name is Elizabeth Morris, and this is my sign name. I am a Deaf actor, I use American Sign Language as a storyteller. I like to interpret music using ASL, as well as theatre. I am also involved in film. In all my roles use ASL, American Sign Language I want people to see me as a visual artist. I am a Deaf artist, also a visual artist. I don't want people to see a disability. I am a culturally Deaf person. I want people to be open-minded and come see my performance. Also rely on the visual experience instead of the auditory. Focus on the visual. The word disability already has an emphasis on the word "dis." That already has a negative connotation. I am Deaf. I don't consider myself disabled. I see myself as a deaf person being part of a culture, which includes language, history, culture, and community, therefore I don't consider myself as having a disability. Though maybe instead of "disabilities" we can think of an alternative word to "disabilities" Maybe "diverse abilities" My eyes are very important and valued as part of my identity. And that makes me see things as a visual artist using ASL as a primary language. ♫

BX9UM5k4yCg.text: COVID-19 TIPS: Wash Your Hands  Hi! Today's date is March 25, 2020. We will get this opportunity to to explain how to wash your hands properly and make sure your hands are sparkle clean. Silent Voice IHP ASL Consultant, Michelle Chung and and Deaf Ontarian student, Lavinia Courtney will explain how to wash your hands Watch! I will show you how to wash your hands I will use black paint Imagine this paint as hand soap Did you see how I wash my hands? You make sure to wash your hands thoroughly with soap. Rub your hands for at least 20 seconds. Make sure your hands is fully covered with soap. When you finish washing your hands, rinse your hands under warm running water. Dry your hands off with a clean towel Your hands are now clean! The first step is to wet your hands. Add soap to your hands Next step is to lather up then rinse with water I finished with the first step and second step. What should I do next? Water is dripping from hands What should I do? I am thinking what to do next. I forget. Dry hands with a clean towel I finish with these steps. I am thinking the next step after washing my hands I finish rinsing my hands I finish rinsing my hands. Umm... I dry my hands. My hands are now sparkling clean. Why is it important to wash your hands? To prevent the virus and germs from spreading I am finished! Protect yourself!

MK03XWZ-JMU.text:
CHS Employment Services - we can support you CHS Employment Services are specialized services which cater to people who are Deaf or hard of hearing which offer support when looking for work. Our services are free-of-charge to job seekers aged 18 and over, and employers looking for qualified staff.

If you or someone you know is Deaf or hard of hearing, unemployed or underemployed, or needs supports and accommodations at work, contact CHS Employment Services. We’re here to support you! Are you Deaf? Hard of hearing? Want a job? CHS Employment Services can help! If you are culturally Deaf, oral deaf, deafened or hard of hearing, CHS Employment Services offers assistance to find a job, and works with employees and employers to put the right supports in place to help you both succeed. Our services are free-of-charge to job seekers aged 18 and over, and employers looking for qualified staff. Andy is hard of hearing. After suffering hearing loss due to noise exposure, Andy needed assistance updating his resume and finding a new career. CHS helped him get a job. Emily is Deaf. After high school, Emily needed support getting her first job. She worked with her CHS employment counsellor to find work as a hostess at Swiss Chalet. CHS helped her get a job. CHS Employment Services can support you with: Career guidance and interview preparation, Resume and cover letter services, Leads and referrals to potential job interviews. CHS Employment Services are specialized services which cater to people who are Deaf or hard of hearing which offer support when looking for work. Our experienced employment consultants are knowledgeable about the unique needs of people who are Deaf or hard of hearing and can communicate in the language of your choice. We can offer suggestions for assistive technology and communication and safety strategies to make the workplace more accessible for Deaf or hard of hearing employees. For Deaf or hard of hearing people with literacy challenges and Deaf immigrants whose first language is not English or ASL, CHS employment counsellors can address those needs as well. If you or someone you know is Deaf or hard of hearing, unemployed or underemployed, or needs supports and accommodations at work, contact CHS Employment Services. We’re here to support you! Contact us today! Phone: 613.521.0509 TTY: 1.888.697.3650 Email: info@chs.ca www.chs.ca/employment-services

S2_88aUJdDM.text: Canada Deaf Youth Film Festival 2020 [ASL]  Hi! You good? How are you? I am doing good. I miss going out and seeing my friends. I feel like I am stuck at home all day and night. I am not sure what I should do now. Me too. I was so bored and decided to browse on my phone. I saw Silent Voice's post about Silent Voice hosting Canada Deaf Youth Film Festival. Have you seen this post? No, I haven't seen it. Tell me more about it! This is cool! This will be a quarantine style film festival. All Deaf youth are required to make their films inside their homes only. Cool! Do I have to film inside the house? Yes, you may film outside of your home as long it is in your front yard or backyard only. You may not use public spaces (i.e. parks, roads, malls, community centres, etc.). Your health and safety are our priority. I want my friends to participate my films. How can I get them participate to my film? You may only include people who live in your home with you in your film project. If you want your friends to participate, there are many ways to do that such as Zoom, FaceTime, Skype and many more! Yes, that's a good idea! What kind of film should I make? We welcome all types of films such as scary, comedy, romance or adventure. Be as creative as you can! Please make sure that your film is G rated and family friendly Are there limits on film submission and time length? You may submit as many films as you would like. If you want to submit more than one film, you will be required to make an individual submission for each film. 3 films = 3 submissions. A film runs up to 5 minutes. Awesome! How do I submit? When is the deadline? You can submit your film to the link below. Please upload your film(s) to YouTube and share your unlisted YouTube link(s) via our website. You are required to get your parents to sign the wavier form and you are done! The deadline is on May 29, 2020. I am curious. Who are the judges? We have 5 wonderful judges who are skilled in filming. Keep your eyes peeled on our social media. We will spotlight each judge on Silent Voice Canada's social media platforms weekly. Stay tuned! Will there be prizes? There will be 3 prizes for each age category: under 12 years old and 13 to 17 years old. Each catgeory will have prizes for 1st place, 2nd place and 3rd place. The winners will be announced online on June 10, 2020. All films submitted will be shared online on Silent Voice’s social media platforms. I want to show my films on my personal account. Can I? You may share your films on your personal accounts. Make sure that you tag Silent Voice Canada on Facebook and Instagram using hashtag #CDYFF2020. Yes, I am going to make a film now! Now it is your turn! I am excited to watch your film!

2021 YLC: iViperfly VIDEO DESCRIPTION AND TRANSCRIPT: Text appears on a black background, "YLC Summer 2021 iViperfly, by team iViperfly" 

Cal is standing by a table with a canvas and paint brushes. Cal looks at her phone. 

CAL: Wow, that painting is so neat. I wish I could do the same. (sigh)

Avery walks her dog and trips over. 

Enow plays basketball and keeps missing the hoop. He sits down, disappointedly. 

Zoe is trying to make something with her beads, looks fustrated. 

ZOE: Ugh! I've been struggling. I do Wow, that painting is so neat. I wish I could do the same. I've been struggling. I don't think I can do this. I give up. The music is bleak. I don't even understand. Ugh. I'm not good at this. Wow I understand this clearly! The music sounds good - I like... I feel like I've been bitten by a snake or something. Ow! I did it! I finally did it. Something bit me. I felt something. (Everyone greets hello.) We need to decide our team name and sign name. Any ideas? Nate, what happened to your arm. Are you hurt? I was on my computer, playing a game. All of sudden, I got a viper bite! (Everyone agrees and says the same.) Hold. I have an idea. We should do a sign with "viper". That's a good idea. I want to add something. "Viper" sounds plain. With today's technology, mac, iphones, why don't we do "iViper"? I think that may not be enough. We should add something. We're all at YLC together. At first, we're like caterpillars because we haven't fully grown. At the end of YLC, we become butterflies. Should we add "fly"? Yeah, we should do this sign in ASL. No, I suggest a different sign because we went through YLC together. I still feel we're missing something. Like Zoe mentioned, in the beginning of the week, we're all new and haven't learned any leadership lessons. At the end of the week, we become experienced and can thrive! How about this?

1so4-zBOcbk.text: Hurricane Ida Food Assstance - ASL  Food assistance is a major issue for residents impacted by disasters. The Louisiana Department of Children and Family Services manages federal programs that provide critical assistance at times like this. One of those programs is Disaster SNAP -- or DSNAP. DCFS has received federal approval for virtual DSNAP for residents in 25 parishes impacted by hurricane Ida. These include all of the parishes here, as well as two ZIP Codes in lower Saint Martin Parish: 70339 and 70380 only. What is DSNAP? DSNAP provides food assistance to eligible households who do not receive regular SNAP benefits... and need help buying groceries due to income or damages following a disaster, like Hurricane Ida. It’s important to know that SNAP recipients are not eligible for DSNAP and should not apply. However, anyone who has applied for SNAP but not yet been approved can still apply for DSNAP. DSNAP starts the week of September 20th. These are the parishes scheduled for Week 1. Residents should check the schedule to determine when DCFS is taking applications for their parish... and then call for their interview according to their last name. For example, A through F last names should call on Monday, G through M last names should call on Tuesday, etcetera. Make sure to take advantage of the A to Z days! If it’s your parish’s week to apply, the A to Z days are bonus days. . Any last name can call. Phase 2 parishes call to apply and interview, beginning on Monday, September 27th. You can check the schedule, find Frequently-Asked Questions and more... on the DCFS website at DCFS.la.gov/DSNAP. Last but not least are the Phase 3 parishes, whose residents can call to apply... and interview beginning on, October 4th. Please remember to check the schedules and mark your calendars. Now that you know when to apply for DSNAP, let’s talk about the process. To be interviewed for DSNAP and establish eligibility, you should be either the Head of Household, the spouse, another responsible household member, or an Authorized Representative (we’ll talk more about that in a minute). This is a “virtual” DSNAP, meaning it’s all done by phone as opposed to the in-person DSNAP operations you may be familiar with. DCFS staff are conducting DSNAP interviews from 7:30 AM to 6 PM. Pay attention to the alphabet schedule, call on your assigned day for your parish, and do take advantage of the A-to-Z days. Dial 888-LAHelpU, that’s 888-524-3578, toll-free, select your language, and then press 6. If you cannot call to complete your interview, you may request... an in-person interview at a DCFS Office... by completing an online form on the DCFS website. There are provisions for Deaf, Deaf-Blind and Hard-of-Hearing applicants to ensure their access to the virtual DSNAP process. Applicants can access DSNAP through Telecommunication Relay Service by dialing 711 on their phones.  Services include Text telephone (or TTY), Voice Carry Over, Hearing Carry Over, Speech-to-Speech, and Visually Assisted Speech-to-Speech. Just call LAHelp-U at 1-888-524-3578, using your VRS provider. Select your language and press 6. For more information about these options, email the Louisiana Commission for the Deaf. They can also use the Video Relay System, or VRS, which allows people who are deaf or hard of hearing to communicate with others through the use of American Sign Language (ASL). As mentioned previously, applicants can name an Authorized Representative, or AR, to apply for DSNAP on their behalf. The Head of Household or another responsible household member must authorize the person to serve as AR for them. The DCFS caseworker taking your case will need to speak to a household member to confirm that they agree for the AR to speak for them. If the applicant or another member of the household cannot speak, to designate the AR, the worker will ask to obtain the designation in writing. This can be done through a secure email or by mail. If the AR has general Power of Attorney for the Head of Household, the interview can be completed with the Power of Attorney without requiring permission from the Head of Household. If the AR does not have general Power of Attorney and is unable to speak to the household to verify that permission was granted for the AR to complete the interview, the household must designate in writing that the AR has permission to complete the interview. The designation must include the name and address of the person chosen as the AR and must be signed by the household member making the authorization. The worker can send an email through a secure email box requesting the applicant designate the AR in writing or provide a copy of the general Power of Attorney. If the applicant does not have an email account, the worker will send a notice requesting the AR Designation.  A DSNAP AR Designation Form will be included. You can find more information on the DCFS website at DCFS.la.gov/DSNAP. Here’s the form DCFS would send if needed. What can you do to prepare for your DSNAP interview and application? DCFS encourages you to pre-register online, but they say it is not required. Either way, you still have to call on your assigned day to interview and apply. Before you do, be sure to gather your information: It will be essential that you have names, dates of birth and Social Security Numbers for each member of the household and the Driver’s license or state-issued ID for the Head of Household. You will need your residential address and mailing address if they are different. You will also need income and resource information for each member of the household. This includes cash on hand, checking and savings account information. Lastly, you will need to provide the amount of disaster-related expenses you incurred or will incur over a 30-day period around the disaster – August 26th through September 24th. Another way to prepare is to download the LA Wallet App which will speed the application process by verifying your identity and establishing your residency. You can learn more about DSNAP on the DCFS website at dcfs.la.gov/DSNAP. You can also text LADSNAP to 898-211 for information and updates. We talked a lot about DSNAP, but we also want to provide information for SNAP recipients. DCFS received federal approval to issue automatic SNAP replacement benefits to SNAP recipients in 18 parishes impacted by Hurricane Ida. These are parishes where at least 50% of the residents lost power for at least 24 hours or more. SNAP recipients from other parishes who lost food purchased with SNAP benefits and lost power for at least 24 hours can also request replacement SNAP benefits by applying on the DCFS website. DCFS has disaster resource links and other information available on its website. Visit dcfs.la.gov/disaster.

Support for Families Initiative Premier Doug Ford and Education Minister Stephen Lecce announced new financial support for families. The support for families initiative is a one-time payment:  $200 for each child up to 12 years of age; and $250 per child up to 21 years of age with special needs. 

Make sure you have the information ready before applying:

- Banking information
- Child’s Birth date
- The name of school (public or private)
- Parent’s email

Parents who received financial support during the school strikes a few m Premier Doug Ford and Education Minister Stephen Lecce have announced new financial support for families. The Support for Families initiative is a one-time only payment: $200 for each child up to 12 years of age, and $250 per child up to 21 years of age with special needs. Make sure you have the following information ready before applying: banking information; your child's date of birth; the name of your child's school (public or private); parent's email address. Parents who received financial support during the school strikes a few months ago do not need to fill out the direct deposit information; the government already has it. If you did not, parents are required to provide their banking information on this website. If you have any questions, please contact me or Rosaria. Thank you.

PAH Virtual Activities Here are some online activities you can access with your kids!

Educational
Virtual Museum Tours: shorturl.at/prBJM 
Dad Lab Science Experiments: shorturl.at/govNX 
Scholastic Learn at Home: shorturl.at/lnozK 

Physical Activity
At Home Workout: https://fast.wistia.net/embed/channel/5jehdru8mr

Animal Live Cams
Jelly Fish Cam: https://www.georgiaaquarium.org/webcam/jelly-webcam/
Panda Cam: https://www.calgaryzoo.com/visit/animals/giant-pandas Hi! I will share cool virtual activities for you. These virtual activities are great tools to keep your kids busy at home. I will share 5 following activities. The first virtual activity is the museum's virtual tour. The virtual tour includes 20 famous museums across the world. You can have a virtual tour at home. The second activity is Dad Lab Science Experiment. This activity provides on-hands activities related to arts, science and wonders. The third activity is Scholastic Learn at Home. They have great projects to keep kids reading, thinking, and growing. These activities are designed for preK to Grade 9. You can browse and find a project that is the best for your child. The fourth activity is At Home Workouts. They have 20 different programs. They are very simple, takes only 5 minutes, and you can work out up to 4 times a day. You will have a happy brain afterwards! SjI4N3nBk3Y.text:
Flash Survey for DDBHH for TNC 2018 246 Retail Sales Practices of Telecoms Flash Survey of Deaf, Deaf-Blind and 
Hard of Hearing Canadians for
Retail sales practices of Canada’s
large telecommunications carriers

TRANSCRIPT - https://docs.google.com/document/d/1XOTP1OoLuGqlsLe7sB6y8HQunCwbeT3oWc4XpQ6I2MM/edit?usp=sharing Hey hey, we need your help!! First let me introduce myself, my name is I'm the chairperson for DWCC - CSSSC, I work with three other organizations and they are DAANS, CAD-ASC, and CNSDB We are doing a limited time flash survey with only 16 questions, takes only 10 minutes. We are only looking for up to 50 respondents who are Deaf, Deaf-Blind and Hard of Hearing. Yes, DWCC group did surveys in past for the CRTC, but this one is different! Therefore this survey has fewer questions and we need to collect true biz evidence. Now I'm going to provide some background. The federal government received many complaints from the Canadian public about large Canadian telecommunications service provider companies and how aggressive or relentless they were when promoting or selling their telephone, wireless or internet services. Public complain blurry not clear, miscommunicate, not tell true biz, cover up… should inform but gave missing information and incomplete information by phone company staff sales agents or technical support or advertisements about what? 1) Your contract is not clear to you. 2) Selling phone company services that are not a proper fit/match for Deaf, Deaf-Blind, and Hard of Hearing (DDBHH) How did the federal government respond after receiving these complaints? It “ordered” the CRTC to follow up on these complaints. CRTC has questions related to persons with disabilities and access groups Including Deaf, Deaf-Blind and Hard of Hearing. Our group wants to gather your experiences and summarize them into one report to CRTC. Your experiences will help CRTC paper-list policy mandate better sales experiences for DDBHH Canadians. That's our vision Did you experience this unclear, gap information, service not match? If yes, then this survey is for you! Submitting information to CRTC for this review has new rules - not seen before. This time, CRTC requires evidence. For example: Date, time location, and how did you communicate? CRTC is also interested to know how you tru biz resolved issue if it did happen. What steps did you take to solve it? If it wasn't solved, that's fine. It will be accepted. It'll show inept customer service in solving the problem. Evidence is required - examples are: 1) Transcripts from IP Relay conversations, 2) Copy pasted or e-mail forward of online Live Chat support conversations, 3) Scanned documents of paper and pen writing back and forth, 4) E-mailed forward or copy and pastes of typed back and forth conversation between you and the sales representative. DWCC group will sort through the respondents and present the best 25 stories (ASL/LSQ, or English/French) to share with the CRTC. At the end of the survey, you may insert a youtube, or Vimeo video of you using ASL/LSQ to share your experience. Let us know what happened and what were your feelings or responses to this experience. How did you communicate? By pen and paper, with or without interpreter, or by VRS, IP Relay, or live chat. Please make your ASL and LSQ video in youtube or Vimeo and have a link the video ready BEFORE starting this survey. It'll help to save time. There are a total just 16 questions in this survey and will take ten minutes of your time! Thank you for your participation! To take this survey, you must be: A) Be a sign language user ASL or LSQ, B) Be 18 years of age or older, C) Be a Canadian resident, D) Deaf, Deaf-Blind, Hard of Hearing, or Late-deafened; and E) Use a phone - wireless, internet, or landline, television or internet services in Canada If you have issues with more than one telecommunications company, please fill out this survey once for EACH company you have an experience with. So we'll know the experience is for each specific company. It'll help us sort through them, them, and give them to the CRTC. This is a public survey and your email addresses will be kept in the strictest confidence. We will require your email address to contact you directly ONLY to verify or clarify details. Your email addresses will not be kept after the survey finishes. Any questions? Feel free to email Thank you so much! If you have evidence but don't want to do the survey and instead rather to email to us, that's fine. You may email us at Thank you so much! Merci beaucoup!

Bellman Visit Pager Receiver Charger - Product SKU: BE1260 Keep your Visit Pager Receiver fully charged and ready to use with the Visit Pager Receiver Charger. You can also connect one Bellman Visit Alarm Clock or up to two Bellman Visit Bedshakers to the charger. The unit uses one 1.5-volt rechargeable battery 
(included). Its adaptor features an internal back-up battery, in case of power outages. When you buy the Bellman Visit Pager Receiver you may wish you could recharge the battery. You can, with this device and the included 1.5-volt rechargeable battery. Rest easy, you can plug in up to two bed shakers, and/or the Bellman Alarm Clock. In the event of a power failure, this device has a battery backup with enough power for the Bellman Bed Shaker.

y0aNd72taVs.text:
COVID-19 Corner https://silentvoiceasll2.wixsite.com/deaf-covid-19 

A new website now available to keep you up-to-date on the latest COVID-19 information!

The information shared on this site is from trusted sources.  Some information is collected by Silent Voice and put into ASL.  There are also videos and links that we feel are important resources to share with our community. Hello! We are excited to share the news Silent Voice is launching our new website The purpose of this website is to provide the latest news and tips on COVID-19. We all practice self quarantine and stay healthy and safe. Where can we find the latest information about COVID-19 in American Sign Language (ASL)? We have decided to launch our new website for you to get updates When you enter our website, you will see 5 tabs on top of the home page The first tab is called, "Updates." These updates are from municipal, provincial, and federal news. You will see different posts and current news under the "Updates" tab All posts are accessible. ASL interpreter included. You can get your information in ASL The second tab is called, "Silent Voice Updates." Our office is close and we work remotely. For further notices, we share our updates under this tab The third tab is called, "Tips and Prevention." There are more restrictions on COVID-19 lately. Staying safe and health is very important. Browse under this tab to get more information on tips and prevention The fourth tab is called, "Activities.' Family stays at home most of time and needs to find some ideas, resources, and information in ASL to keep themselves busy You can browse more information in ASL under this tab to keep your family busy. The fifth tab is called, "Kid Activities." All posts are in ASL for kids to keep themselves busy such as watching ASL storytime, learning, exercising, playing activities and many more. These posts are designed for kids only. All posts, expect for Silent Voice Updates, are shared from the community. We collect all shared posts in one website. What is the best part of this website? Everything is in ASL and accessible for all. It helps you to understand better. Take a look at our website! Help yourself browse for more information, tips, ideas and resources!

QDyZTPBxT2w.text:
Bring Accepted ID to Vote (ASL) | Elections Canada Descriptive Video: 
VISUAL: Federal Election appears. Fade to black.
NARRATOR: Federal election. Bring your ID when you go to vote. 
SUPER: Bring accepted ID to vote
VISUAL: Title + Elections Canada logo.
NARRATOR: To vote, you must be a Canadian citizen, at least 18 years old on election day and prove your identity and current address.
VISUAL: Title card fades. Canadian leaf appears. Character appears and the leaf settles in front of their chest. There is a text box to the right indicating 18+. Bring your ID when you go to vote. To vote, you must be a Canadian citizen, at least 18 years old on election day and prove your identity and current address. You can show one ID card issued by the provincial, territorial or federal government. It should have your photo, name and current address. For example: your driver’s licence. Or you can show two pieces of ID. Both must have your name, and at least one must have your current address. For example: your voter information card and a bank statement. We accept e-statements and e-invoices. Print them or show them on a mobile device. If you don’t have ID, you can still vote by declaring your identity and address in writing. Someone who knows you will have to vouch for you in person. They must be assigned to your polling station and prove their own identity and address. You don’t need to remove your mask when proving your identity. Visit elections.ca for the official information on voting and the health and safety measures in place or call 1-800-463-6868 (TTY 1-800-361-8935).

p4mEk6xdFS0.text: COVID-19 Update: Bill C-13, the COVID-19 Emergency Response Act on March 25, 2020  On March 25, PM Justin Trudeau's message Bill C-13, the COVID-19 Emergency Response Act, was passed in the House of Commons on March 25 at 6:00am. The Act is nwo being debated in the Senate and is expected to receive royal assent later in the afternoon of March 26. The legislation includes: Canada Emergency Response Benefit (CERB), which provides $2,000/month, for the next four months. The benefit is to workers who lose their income because of COVID-19. The benefit also applies to people who are self-employed, are sick or quarantined, must stay home to look after children, still have a job but are not receiving an income. The benefit combines the previously announced Emergency Care Benefit and the Emergency Support Benefit. Prime Minister Trudeau said the government aims to have an online portal live by April 6 and cheques out to Canadians within 10 days of applying. Also, the legislation includes: removing the government's ability to raise taxes unilaterally. Shortening the period in which the government can take emergency spending measures without Parliament's approval from 6 months to 2 years for specific categories including income support for Canadians, the purchase of medical supplies, public health-related programs, covering expenses incurred by the government and helping provinces and territories pay for emergency response needs. Provinces and territories are also an extra $500 million in federal funding. The ability to authorize the borrowing of money without Parliament's approval if it is needed to "maintain the efficiency of the financial system in Canada." This authority, too, is only valid through the end of September and and requires report to MPs within 30 days of any borrowing. Also, MPs agreed to a motion that included: adjourning the House of Commons until April 20 with the option to extend longer if it is in the public interest to stay adjourned or to be recalled to consider additional measures. Allowing the Standing Committees of Finance and Health to convene (remotely) once a week to review evidence concerning the government's response Requiring the Finance Minister to report on a bi-weekly basis to the finance committee. The House of Commons Finance committee will conduct a study of the Emergency Response Act within 6 months. The Prime Minister also announced that the government will be providing additional measures to support journalists and media companies in Canada during the crisis.
